tree.setup: Six-thirty in the morning. A street-side baozi stand with bamboo steamers stacked four high, each one breathing out little puffs of steam. Auntie Fang works with terrifying efficiency -- she can make change, fill a bag, and shout an order simultaneously. There's already a line of commuters.
tree.setup_zh: 早上六点半。路边的包子铺，竹蒸笼叠了四层，每层都冒着热气。方阿姨干活又快又准——她能同时找钱、装袋、喊号。上班的人已经排队了。

turns.speaker: npc
turns.text_en: What do you want? Hurry up!

options.feedback: Auntie Fang's tongs move like lightning. Two perfect buns land in a paper bag before you finish blinking. Short, clear orders are exactly what she wants during rush hour.

options.text_pinyin: nǐ hǎo, qǐngwèn yǒu shénme bāozi?
options.text_en: Hello, what kinds of baozi do you have?
options.feedback: Polite, but the line behind you grows restless. Auntie Fang speed-lists:「肉的、菜的、豆沙的！」At a morning stall, keep it quick.
The man behind you sighs audibly. Auntie Fang gives you exactly three seconds of patience before serving the next person. Know your order before you reach the front!
Auntie Fang ladles hot soy milk into a paper cup with zero spillage. Baozi and 豆浆 is one of China's great breakfast combinations. 杯 (bēi) is the measure word for cups.
Auntie Fang nods without judgment. She's already moved on to the next customer. Efficiency is her love language.

options.text_en: How much is the soy milk?
options.feedback: 「两块！」Auntie Fang answers without looking up. At street stalls, prices are usually so low that asking feels almost ceremonial.

turns.speaker: npc
turns.text_en: Six yuan total. See you tomorrow!

tree.cultural_note: 包子 (bāozi) with 豆浆 (dòujiāng, soy milk) is a classic Chinese breakfast. Street stalls open before dawn and close by mid-morning. The pace is fast -- regulars order in three words or fewer.
